file_name: Blackledge - 2018 - School of electrical and electronic engineering 2011-0501 moletest  a web-based skin cancer screeni.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper describes Moletest, a web-based skin cancer screening system that uses an expert system based on image analysis algorithms. Specifically, it employs fractal geometry to quantify object texture and border irregularity, Euclidean geometry for shape and asymmetry, and a Fuzzy Logic decision-making engine to classify moles as benign or malignant based on feature vectors derived from digital images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Conference paper' and reports on original research and development work. It details the design, algorithms (fractal and Euclidean geometry, fuzzy logic), and implementation of a novel web-based skin cancer screening system called Moletest. The abstract states it 'reports on a research and development programme' and describes the methods and results of this new technology, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Moletest: A Web-based Skin Cancer Screening System
brief_summary: This paper describes Moletest, the world's first web-based remote skin cancer screening system developed by Dublin Institute of Technology and Moletest UK Limited. The system utilizes an expert system that analyzes high-quality digital images of moles uploaded by users to classify them as benign or malignant. The AI methods employed include fractal geometry to quantify texture and border irregularity, Euclidean geometry for shape and asymmetry, and a Fuzzy Logic decision-making engine to process feature vectors. The system was trained using a knowledge database created from expert-reviewed case-study images and is designed to filter benign cases before they reach general practitioners. The study reports an accuracy of over 90% for the diagnosis, attributing this performance to the effective application of fractal geometry for characterizing textured objects. The service launched in September 2010 and aims to improve early detection rates for melanoma while reducing the burden on healthcare providers.

lesion_types_studied.details: The system classifies moles as benign or malignant, with a specific focus on screening for malignant melanoma, which is described as the most serious form of skin cancer. The system aims to increase early detection of malignant melanoma.
The system filters benign cases (normal moles) to reduce the burden on healthcare providers. The output provides a decision as to whether the object is 'normal' or 'abnormal'.

Mentioned in the context of screening for both non-melanoma and melanoma skin cancer, and noting that non-melanoma skin cancer is common in people who spend time outdoors. However, the specific algorithms and training described focus on classifying moles as benign or malignant (melanoma).

The system is designed to analyze the structure of a mole or other skin 'defects' and detect cancer-identifying features. It also handles cases with 'foreign objects' in manual segmentation mode, but these are not the primary diagnostic targets.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant (Normal vs. Cancerous Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The system classifies moles as benign or malignant (normal or cancerous melanoma) using a Fuzzy Logic decision-making engine. The output is a categorical decision ('normal', 'borderline', or 'cancerous') based on feature vectors derived from Euclidean and fractal geometric parameters. The paper explicitly states the system makes a 'decision' as to whether the object is 'normal' or 'abnormal' and assigns a probability to a predefined set of classes.

datasets_used.name: Moletest Knowledge Database (KDB)
datasets_used.description: A knowledge database created by dermatologists using case-study images to train the expert system. It is described as 'continuously evolving' and 'improving' with use, incorporating comparisons with later clinical diagnoses from biopsies and other examinations to inform future analyses.

use.training: Used to train the fuzzy logic decision-making engine via case-study images provided by dermatologists.
use.other: The system continues to 'learn' by comparing its findings with later clinical diagnoses of dermatologists following biopsies and other examinations.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports an accuracy of over 90% based on a knowledge database and clinical comparisons, but does not provide statistical significance testing.

Moletest Knowledge Database (KDB).notes: The accuracy of 90%++ is attributed primarily to the application of fractal geometry for characterizing objects that are innately textural. The system uses a 42-element feature vector composed of Euclidean and fractal geometric parameters to train the fuzzy inference engine. The system outputs a decision ('normal', 'borderline', or 'cancerous') based on this database. The system continues to 'learn' by comparing its findings with later clinical diagnoses of dermatologists following biopsies and other examinations.

architecture_used.main_model: Expert system utilizing a Fuzzy Logic decision-making engine
architecture_used.pretraining: Supervised training using a Knowledge Database (KDB) created by dermatologists from case-study images; the system continues to 'learn' by comparing findings with later clinical diagnoses.
architecture_used.input_size: Good quality digital images (specific dimensions not reported)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 42-element feature vector composed of Euclidean geometric parameters (area, perimeter, centre of gravity, shape, asymmetry) and fractal geometric parameters (Fractal Dimension, Lacunarity) for texture and border irregularity.
processing_steps: Feature Detection (computation of Euclidean and fractal parameters)
architecture_details.logic_type: Fuzzy Sets / Fuzzy Logic Inference Engine

training_details.method: Supervised training with expert-reviewed case-study images

classification_or_output_head.output: Decision ('normal', 'borderline', or 'cancerous') with an estimate of precision/probability

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Expert system based on image analysis algorithms
preprocessing_techniques: Wiener filtering to reduce noise and remove unnecessary features such as light flecks
Edge detection using an application-specific filter to identify regions of interest
Segmentation (Automatic mode identifying the largest/darkest object; Manual mode for user-selected areas of interest)
conclusions: The Moletest system successfully implements a web-based remote skin cancer screening service that classifies moles as benign or malignant using an expert system based on fractal and Euclidean geometric analysis combined with a Fuzzy Logic decision-making engine.
The application of fractal geometry for characterizing textured objects is identified as a key innovation that enables high accuracy and automation in the screening process.
The system achieves an accuracy of over 90% in diagnosing melanoma, attributed to the effective use of fractal geometry and a 42-element feature vector derived from Euclidean and fractal parameters.
The system is designed to filter benign cases, thereby reducing the burden on general practitioners and healthcare providers by allowing them to focus on higher-risk patients.
The reliability of the system is fundamentally dependent on the quality of the input image data; poor visibility, resolution, or contour quality can lead to system failure.
A significant limitation is the computational intensity of the method, where increasing accuracy may be incompatible with acceptable computing costs and real-time hardware requirements.
The system's performance is sensitive to the distribution of training data; non-uniformly distributed training objects can prevent the generation of accurate results.
The system continues to 'learn' and improve by comparing its automated findings with later clinical diagnoses from dermatologists following biopsies and other examinations.
important_remarks: The reported accuracy of '90%++' lacks statistical rigor; no p-values, confidence intervals, or formal hypothesis testing are provided.
The system's reliability is critically dependent on input image quality; poor visibility, resolution, or contour quality can lead to system failure.
There is a significant trade-off between accuracy and computational cost; increasing accuracy may be incompatible with acceptable computing costs and real-time hardware requirements.
Performance is sensitive to training data distribution; non-uniformly distributed training objects can prevent the generation of accurate results.

The evaluation relies on a 'continuously evolving' Knowledge Database (KDB) rather than a fixed, independent test set, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific expert-reviewed cases.
The system was not validated against classical correlative techniques or dermatoscopy, which are noted as having higher potential accuracy.

The study population bias is implicit in the reliance on a knowledge database created by dermatologists, which may not represent the full diversity of skin types or lesion presentations in the general population.
The system is designed to filter benign cases to reduce GP burden, but the clinical relevance of its 'borderline' category and the consequences of false negatives are not detailed.
